name: Long QT Syndrome
identifyBy: QTc >470ms in women, >450ms in men (Bazett formula)
T wave may be: abnormally wide, biphasic, notched, or late-peaking
Drug-induced: T wave becomes broad and bizarre; may develop notching
Congenital LQTS type 1: broad-based T wave; Type 2: biphasic T; Type 3: long isoelectric ST then peaked T
Risk of Torsades de Pointes - 'twisting of the points' polymorphic VT

measurements.rate: 60–80 bpm (measured at slow HR - QTc corrects for rate)
measurements.stSegment: Normal (ST segment may be prolonged in congenital LQTS type 3)

management: Discontinue all QT-prolonging drugs: antipsychotics, antibiotics (azithromycin, ciprofloxacin), antifungals, metoclopramide - check CredibleMeds database
Correct electrolytes: hypokalaemia and hypomagnesaemia are major contributors
IV magnesium sulphate 2g over 10 min: drug-induced long QT with Torsades
Torsades de Pointes: MgSO4, correct electrolytes, increase HR (overdrive pacing or isoprenaline to shorten QT)
Congenital LQTS: beta-blockers (LQTS1 and 2), mexiletine (LQTS3), ICD if high risk
Avoid competitive sports, triggers (LQTS1: swimming/exercise; LQTS2: auditory stimuli)
pearls: QTc >500ms: very high risk - Torsades risk increases sharply above this threshold
Bazett formula (QTc = QT/√RR) overcorrects at high HR - use Fridericia (cube root) for tachycardia
Drug checklist: EVERY new medication should be checked for QTc prolongation potential - CredibleMeds.org
Hypokalaemia + QT-prolonging drug = markedly elevated risk (synergistic)
Congenital vs drug-induced: family history, syncope on exertion or loud noise, gene testing for congenital LQTS
differentials: Hypokalaemia long QT (prominent U waves, flat T)
Hypocalcaemia long QT (prolonged flat ST, normal T shape)
Complete heart block (long RR, may have long apparent QT)
Normal QT at slow HR (always use QTc - heart rate corrected value)
